build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: Story advances as paced dialogue/narration the player drives forward (clicking or a key reveals the next line) over recognizable academy scenes, with a readable speaker, rather than dumping all text at once or auto-scrolling. Strong submissions integrate dialogue with visible characters, scene changes, and storybook framing; a mostly empty screen with a dialogue box should be judged low even if the text advances correctly. Score 0 if there is no readable advancing dialogue system, if text appears all at once with no pacing, or if the player cannot control progression.

Training is an actual player action with scarcity: between story beats the player chooses which magical discipline to train, and doing so visibly raises a tracked ability/stat while consuming a limited resource (time, energy, action points, or term slots) so investing in one pursuit means giving up another. Attempting to train with no resource left clearly fails or is unavailable. Score 0 if there is no training action, if abilities never change, if training is unlimited with no cost, or if 'training' is a label that does nothing.

The trained stats are persistent state that the game carries across the term and that later content checks: a story option, a line of dialogue, an event, or which ending is reachable depends on whether a stat is high enough. A later beat must demonstrably gate on the player's built-up stats, not just the most recent click. Score 0 if stats never affect anything beyond a number on screen, if every path is available regardless of training, or if the story is effectively linear with no stat checks.

At story beats the game presents real choices among two or more distinct options, the player selects one, and the story visibly continues down the path that choice implies. Different options should produce different authored beats that can be seen in the resulting scene, dialogue, stat-gated option, character reaction, or ending path. Score 0 if there are no choices, if all options lead to identical content, or if selecting an option has no observable effect.

The full loop is connected without restarting the executable: title -> term begins -> alternate training and story beats over time -> reach an ending -> return to title or start a new term to play again. Every transition is reachable through in-game UI, time/term progression advances, and reaching an ending halts progression on a styled result screen. Score 0 if any transition is missing or broken, if the term cannot reach an ending, or if replaying requires relaunching the application.

There are several distinct magical disciplines to train (elemental, runecraft, alchemy, forbidden arts, or similar), each a separate tracked ability with its own identity, and they are not reskins of one stat — investing in different disciplines builds meaningfully different mages. Different disciplines should lead toward different story possibilities, not only different labels or colors. Score 0 if there is only one trainable stat, or if disciplines all feed one identical value.

Stat thresholds genuinely gate content: across the demos at least two different stat-dependent outcomes are observable — an option, scene, line, or ending available to a mage who trained one way but closed to another. A diligent runescribe and a forbidden-arts dabbler should reach materially different content over more than a single swapped sentence. Score 0 if stats never unlock or block anything, or if all content is reachable no matter how the player trained.

The term reaches multiple distinct endings (honored graduate, fallen to forbidden arts, expelled, hidden truth, etc.) meaningfully different in outcome and tied to how the player trained and chose. At least two different endings are observable across the demos as authored conclusions reached through different play paths, not isolated debug screens or the same ending retitled. Score 0 if there is only one possible ending or no real ending.

The world has narrative breadth: recognizable recurring characters (classmates, mentors, a rival) and several distinct story beats — a rivalry, a mentor's offer, a forbidden temptation, a crisis — each with its own situation and stakes, so the term has varied material rather than one scene repeated. Different beats should pose different dilemmas through playable scenes, not just titles, labels, or short exposition snippets. Score 0 if there is only a single short scene with no breadth.

The writing delivers an authored magic-school story with real stakes: scenes have characters with voice, the consequences of training and choices are felt in the prose, and tempting or dangerous paths can lead to darker outcomes. The content should read as sustained authored prose with scene texture, escalation, and character reactions; a few strong isolated lines should not carry an otherwise thin demo. Score 0 if there is no actual narrative content or only lorem-style placeholder text.

Core reading and planning UI is clear and stable at 1280x720: dialogue/narration text is legible in a framed box or panel with an identifiable speaker, and the training/planning screen clearly shows the disciplines, the player's current stats, and the remaining time/resource so the player can plan. Nothing critical overlaps or is clipped. Score 0 if dialogue, stats, or the training options are unreadable, overlapping, or clipped.

Player input produces clear, immediate feedback: training visibly moves the relevant stat and spends the resource, selecting a choice shows a selected/highlighted state and is acknowledged, and advancing a line changes the text, scene, speaker, portrait, stat state, or choice state. Feedback limited to instant text replacement in the same static layout should be judged low. Score 0 if input produces no visible response, or if it is impossible to tell that training or a choice registered.

Structure is legible: the player can tell where they are — title vs. training/planning vs. story scene vs. ending — and transitions between phases and into endings are clear rather than abrupt jumps with no framing. Endings read unmistakably as endings with their own authored presentation. Score 0 if phases are visually indistinguishable or transitions are confusing.

Overall visual style is coherent and authored across title, training, scenes, and endings: a consistent warm magic-academy art direction with a deliberate palette, typography, and framing. The game should look like a published visual novel, not a debug screen of text and buttons. Strong submissions rely on real illustrated or pixel-art treatment; a scene built mainly from ColorRects, circles, silhouettes, and flat primitive shapes should be judged low even if the colors are coherent. Score 0 if the screen is dominated by default Godot widgets, untextured rectangles, or placeholder text.

Backgrounds and characters use real illustrated or pixel-art assets: scenes have illustrated academy backdrops (classrooms, libraries, halls) and characters are shown with sprites/portraits that establish who is present, where the scene takes place, and the mood. Bare colored shapes, circles as character heads, empty backdrops, or simple geometric stand-ins should receive little credit. Score 0 if scenes have no background art and characters are absent or are plain rectangles/circles.

The dialogue box, name plates, the training/stats screen, choice menus, title, and ending screens use styled, themed UI — illustrated frames, ornamented panels, custom or styled fonts, stat bars or icons fitting the magic-academy theme — rather than raw default Godot Label/Button/Panel defaults or plain flat boxes. UI polish should make the game feel like a magic-school interface, not just readable text containers. Score 0 if primary UI is obviously unstyled default widgets.

Presentation has motion and polish appropriate to a visual novel: text reveals progressively, stat changes animate (a bar filling, a number counting up), scene/character transitions use fades or slides, and there is atmosphere rather than instant hard cuts everywhere. Motion should support the mood beyond text appearing in a box. Score 0 if nothing on screen ever animates and every change is an instant snap.
